title: 3Sum
description: Find all unique triplets in an array that sum to zero

examples.explanation:
Array: [0,0,0], n=3
Loop i=0 (nums[0]=0):
  Loop j=1 (nums[1]=0):
    Loop k=2 (nums[2]=0): sum=0+0+0=0 → add sorted([0,0,0])=(0,0,0)
Result set: {(0,0,0)}
Convert to list: [[0,0,0]]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: Check all possible triplets in the array using three nested loops, filtering those that sum to zero and removing duplicates by sorting and storing in a set. Use this only for small inputs due to inefficiency.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of triplets, but O(n³) in worst case due to set storage

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force 3Sum Solution
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using three nested loops to find all unique triplets summing to zero

examples.trace:
Array: [0,0,0], n=3
Loop i=0 (nums[0]=0):
  Loop j=1 (nums[1]=0):
    Loop k=2 (nums[2]=0): sum=0+0+0=0 → add sorted([0,0,0])=(0,0,0)
Result set: {(0,0,0)}
Convert to list: [[0,0,0]]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Triple Nested Loop Structure
ladders.desc: Generate all unique combinations of three indices using nested loops

examples.trace:
Array: [5,5], n=2
Loop i from 0 to n-3 = 0 to -1: no iterations
Result: []

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking Triplet Sum Equals Zero
ladders.desc: Determine if three numbers sum to zero and handle result storage
ladders.explanation:
The core logic of the brute force 3Sum approach is checking whether three specific numbers sum to zero and properly storing valid results. The intuition is simple arithmetic: for any three numbers a, b, and c, we need to verify if a + b + c = 0. This works because it's the fundamental condition of the 3Sum problem. The algorithm involves: given three array elements nums[i], nums[j], and nums[k], compute their sum. If the sum equals zero, we need to store this triplet. However, we must handle duplicates carefully. Since the same three numbers might appear in different orders in the array (like [-1,0,1] and [0,1,-1]), we sort the three values before storing them. We use a set data structure to automatically eliminate duplicates, as sets don't allow identical elements. The step-by-step process is: 1) Calculate sum = nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k], 2) If sum == 0, create a list [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]], 3) Sort this list, 4) Convert to a tuple (since lists aren't hashable for sets), 5) Add to result_set. The time complexity for this operation is O(1) for the sum check plus O(1) for the sorting (since we're only sorting 3 elements). The space complexity is O(1) for the temporary variables. This connects to the building block (L3) which is sorting small lists. Common mistakes include: forgetting to sort before adding to set (causing duplicates), not converting to tuple (causing type errors with sets), and incorrect sum calculation. The real code pattern involves a simple if-statement followed by sorting and set addition.

Pseudocode:
function processTriplet(nums, i, j, k, result_set):
    if nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0:
        triplet = [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]]
        sorted_triplet = sorted(triplet)
        result_set.add(tuple(sorted_triplet))

examples.trace:
nums[0] = -1, nums[1] = 0, nums[2] = 1
Sum = -1 + 0 + 1 = 0
Triplet list = [-1, 0, 1]
Sorted triplet = [-1, 0, 1]
Tuple = (-1, 0, 1)
Add to result_set: {(-1, 0, 1)}

nums[0] = 2, nums[1] = 3, nums[2] = 4
Sum = 2 + 3 + 4 = 9 ≠ 0
No addition to result_set
Result_set remains {(-1,0,1)}

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting Small Lists and Tuple Conversion
ladders.desc: Sort a 3-element list and convert it to a tuple for set storage

examples.trace:
Create list: [3, 1, 2]
Sort list: compare 3 and 1 → swap: [1, 3, 2]
Compare 3 and 2 → swap: [1, 2, 3]
Compare 1 and 2 → already ordered
Convert to tuple: (1, 2, 3)

Create list: [-1, 0, -1]
Sort list: compare -1 and 0 → already ordered
Compare 0 and -1 → swap: [-1, -1, 0]
Compare -1 and -1 → equal, no swap
Convert to tuple: (-1, -1, 0)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Determine the relative order of two numbers for sorting

examples.trace:
Check a < b: 5 < 3 is False
Check a > b: 5 > 3 is True
Therefore a is greater than b

Check a < b: -2 < -2 is False
Check a > b: -2 > -2 is False
Therefore a equals b

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Numbers and Addition
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic: what numbers are and how to add them
ladders.explanation:
This is the most fundamental concept required for the 3Sum problem: understanding what numbers are and how to perform addition. The intuition is that numbers represent quantities, and addition combines quantities. This works because numbers follow arithmetic rules that are consistent and predictable. We need to understand: 1) What numbers are (integers in this case, which can be positive, negative, or zero), 2) How to add two numbers (combining their values), 3) How to add three numbers (adding two, then adding the third to the result), 4) The concept of zero as the additive identity (any number plus zero equals the number itself). The algorithm for adding three numbers a, b, c is: first compute sum1 = a + b, then compute total = sum1 + c. Alternatively, we can compute total = a + b + c directly if our language supports it. The step-by-step process: identify the values of a, b, and c, apply the addition operation, and obtain the result. For example, to add -1, 0, and 1: -1 + 0 = -1, then -1 + 1 = 0. The time complexity is O(1) for basic arithmetic. The space complexity is O(1). This connects to the basic operation (L4) of comparing numbers, as comparison relies on understanding numerical values. Common mistakes include: confusing addition with other operations, mishandling negative numbers, and order of operations errors. The real code pattern is simply using the + operator.

Pseudocode:
function addThree(a, b, c):
    return a + b + c

examples.trace:
Step 1: Add a and b: 2 + 3 = 5
Step 2: Add result to c: 5 + 4 = 9
Or directly: 2 + 3 + 4 = 9

Step 1: Add a and b: -1 + 1 = 0
Step 2: Add result to c: 0 + 0 = 0
Or directly: -1 + 1 + 0 = 0

approaches.name: HashMap O(n²)
approaches.summary: Use a hash map to store elements and their indices, then iterate with two loops to find pairs that, combined with a third element from the map, sum to zero, handling duplicates by sorting results. It reduces time by avoiding the third nested loop.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the hash map and O(k) for output

ladders.title: Full Problem — 3Sum with HashMap O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using hash map to find all unique triplets summing to zero
ladders.explanation: The HashMap approach to 3Sum leverages the efficiency of O(1) lookups to avoid a third nested loop. The intuition is: for each pair (i, j), we can check if the complement -(nums[i] + nums[j]) exists in a hash map. However, we must handle duplicates carefully since the problem requires unique triplets. We first create a hash map storing each element's last occurrence index. Then we iterate with two nested loops (i from 0 to n-2, j from i+1 to n-1). For each pair, we calculate target = -(nums[i] + nums[j]) and check if target exists in the map with index > j (to ensure we're using a third element that hasn't been used in this pair). To ensure uniqueness, we sort each found triplet and store it in a set. The time complexity is O(n²) for the nested loops plus O(k log 3) for sorting triplets (where k is number of triplets), which simplifies to O(n²). Space complexity is O(n) for the hash map plus O(k) for output storage. This approach is more intuitive than the two-pointer method but requires careful duplicate handling. Common mistakes include: not checking that the third element's index is greater than j (which could reuse elements), forgetting to handle duplicate triplets, and inefficiently rebuilding the hash map. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the key subroutine of finding complement pairs within the hash map framework.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build hash map { -1:4, 0:1, 1:2, 2:3, -4:5 } (storing last indices). Step 2: Initialize result set. Step 3: Outer loop i=0, nums[0]=-1. Inner loop j=1, nums[1]=0. target = -(-1+0) = 1. Check map: 1 exists at index 2 > j=1 → valid. Triplet [-1,0,1]. Sort → [-1,0,1]. Add to set. Step 4: j=2, nums[2]=1. target = -(-1+1) = 0. Check map: 0 exists at index 1 but index=1 ≤ j=2 → invalid (would reuse element). Step 5: j=3, nums[3]=2. target = -(-1+2) = -1. Check map: -1 exists at index 4 > j=3 → valid. Triplet [-1,2,-1]. Sort → [-1,-1,2]. Add to set. Step 6: j=4, nums[4]=-1. target = -(-1+-1) = 2. Check map: 2 exists at index 3 but index=3 ≤ j=4 → invalid. Step 7: j=5, nums[5]=-4. target = -(-1+-4) = 5. Not in map. Step 8: Continue outer loop i=1,2,3,4 (similar checks). Final result: [[-1,-1,2],[-1,0,1]].

Step 1: Build hash map { 0:2 }. Step 2: Initialize result set. Step 3: Outer loop i=0, nums[0]=0. Inner loop j=1, nums[1]=0. target = -(0+0) = 0. Check map: 0 exists at index 2 > j=1 → valid. Triplet [0,0,0]. Sort → [0,0,0]. Add to set. Step 4: j=2, nums[2]=0. target = -(0+0) = 0. Check map: 0 exists at index 2 but index=2 ≤ j=2 → invalid. Step 5: Outer loop i=1, j=2: target = -(0+0) = 0. Check map: 0 exists at index 2 but index=2 ≤ j=2 → invalid. Final result: [[0,0,0]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Complement Pairs with HashMap
ladders.desc: Given an array and target sum, find all pairs (i,j) where nums[i] + nums[j] = target using hash map
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the HashMap 3Sum approach. The intuition is to use a hash map to store elements we've seen, allowing us to find complements in O(1) time. For each element nums[j], we check if (target - nums[j]) exists in the hash map. If it does, we've found a pair. We store indices in the map to avoid using the same element twice. The algorithm: 1. Initialize empty result list and empty hash map. 2. Iterate j from 0 to n-1: a. Calculate complement = target - nums[j]. b. If complement exists in map, get its index i. Add pair (i,j) to results. c. Add nums[j] to map with index j (overwriting previous entry is okay for this sub-routine). Time complexity: O(n) for single pass. Space complexity: O(n) for hash map. This feeds into L0 by being the mechanism to find the third element: in 3Sum, for each pair (i,j), we set target = -(nums[i]+nums[j]) and use this sub-routine to find if target exists with index > j. Common mistakes: not handling duplicate pairs correctly (this sub-routine as written will find all pairs, but for 3Sum we need to control duplicates at higher level), and adding current element to map before checking complement (which could cause self-pairing). The real code pattern: def find_pairs(nums, target): result=[]; seen={}; for j in range(len(nums)): complement=target-nums[j]; if complement in seen: result.append([seen[complement], j]); seen[nums[j]]=j; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result=[], seen={}. Step 2: j=0, nums[0]=2. complement=9-2=7. 7 not in seen. seen[2]=0. Step 3: j=1, nums[1]=7. complement=9-7=2. 2 in seen at index 0. Add pair [0,1] to result. seen[7]=1. Step 4: j=2, nums[2]=11. complement=9-11=-2. -2 not in seen. seen[11]=2. Step 5: j=3, nums[3]=15. complement=9-15=-6. -6 not in seen. seen[15]=3. Final result: [[0,1]].

Step 1: result=[], seen={}. Step 2: j=0, nums[0]=3. complement=6-3=3. 3 not in seen. seen[3]=0. Step 3: j=1, nums[1]=3. complement=6-3=3. 3 in seen at index 0. Add pair [0,1] to result. seen[3]=1. Final result: [[0,1]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using HashMap for O(1) Complement Lookup
ladders.desc: The technique of storing elements in hash map for instant lookup of complements
ladders.explanation: This core logic enables efficient pair finding by trading space for time. The intuition: instead of checking all possible pairs with nested loops (O(n²)), we can store elements as we iterate and check if the complement (target - current) exists in our stored collection. A hash map provides O(1) average-case lookup, making this O(n). The algorithm: 1. Create empty hash map. 2. For each element x at index i: a. Calculate complement = target - x. b. If complement is in map, we have a pair. c. Add x to map with index i. The key insight is that by storing elements as we go, we can 'look back' at previous elements instantly. This is fundamentally different from brute force because it uses memory to avoid redundant comparisons. Time: O(n) for single pass. Space: O(n) for hash map. This feeds into L1 as the mechanism for the pair-finding sub-routine. Common mistakes: using a hash set instead of map (loses index information needed for verification), not handling duplicates properly (map stores last occurrence, which is sufficient for this logic), and misunderstanding that we add after checking (to avoid pairing element with itself). The real code pattern: seen={}; for i,num in enumerate(nums): complement=target-num; if complement in seen: # found pair; seen[num]=i.

examples.trace: Step 1: seen={}. Step 2: i=0, num=10. complement=40-10=30. 30 not in seen. seen[10]=0. Step 3: i=1, num=20. complement=40-20=20. 20 not in seen. seen[20]=1. Step 4: i=2, num=30. complement=40-30=10. 10 in seen at index 0. Found pair (0,2). seen[30]=2.
Step 1: seen={}. Step 2: i=0, num=5. complement=10-5=5. 5 not in seen. seen[5]=0. Step 3: i=1, num=5. complement=10-5=5. 5 in seen at index 0. Found pair (0,1). seen[5]=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Hash Map Index Storage and Lookup
ladders.desc: Storing array elements with their indices in a hash map for efficient retrieval
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating and using a hash map where keys are array elements and values are their indices. The intuition: by mapping each element to its position, we can quickly check if a specific value exists in the array and where it's located. This is crucial for the complement lookup because we need both existence and index verification. The algorithm: 1. Initialize empty dictionary (hash map). 2. Iterate through array with index i: a. Use element as key, store i as value. If duplicate elements exist, we typically store the last occurrence (which works for our use case). 3. To lookup: check if key exists in dictionary, retrieve its index. Time complexity: O(n) to build, O(1) average for lookup. Space: O(n). This feeds into L2 by providing the data structure for complement lookup. Common mistakes: using a hash set (which doesn't store indices), not handling duplicates correctly (storing first vs last index depends on problem requirements), and confusing keys with values. The real code pattern: index_map={}; for i,num in enumerate(nums): index_map[num]=i; # Lookup: if target in index_map: idx=index_map[target].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty map. Step 2: i=0, num=10 → map[10]=0. Step 3: i=1, num=20 → map[20]=1. Step 4: i=2, num=30 → map[30]=2. Step 5: i=3, num=20 → map[20]=3 (overwrites previous). Final map: {10:0, 20:3, 30:2}.
Step 1: Empty map. Step 2: i=0, num=5 → map[5]=0. Step 3: i=1, num=5 → map[5]=1. Step 4: i=2, num=5 → map[5]=2. Final map: {5:2} (only last index stored).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Dictionary Insertion and Lookup
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of adding key-value pairs to a dictionary and checking key existence
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the essential dictionary (hash map) operations used throughout the approach. The intuition: a dictionary allows us to store data as key-value pairs with fast access. Insertion adds a new pair or updates an existing key. Lookup checks if a key exists and retrieves its value. These are O(1) average time operations due to hashing. The algorithm: 1. Insertion: dict[key] = value. If key already exists, value is overwritten. 2. Lookup: if key in dict: value = dict[key]. For our use case, keys are array elements, values are indices. This feeds into L3 as the mechanism for building and querying the index map. Common mistakes: using dict.get() without understanding its return for missing keys, confusing in operator with direct access (which raises KeyError), and not realizing insertion overwrites. The real code pattern: d={}; d['a']=1; # Insert; if 'a' in d: x=d['a']; # Lookup.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty dict d={}. Step 2: Insert: d[5]=10. Dict becomes {5:10}. Step 3: Lookup: check if 5 in d → True. Retrieve d[5]=10.

examples.input: Insert: key='x', value=100. Then insert key='x', value=200. Lookup key='x'.
examples.trace: Step 1: d={}. Step 2: First insert: d['x']=100 → {'x':100}. Step 3: Second insert: d['x']=200 → {'x':200} (overwrites). Step 4: Lookup: 'x' in d → True. d['x']=200.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as ordered collections accessible by numerical indices
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers arrays (or lists), which are ordered collections of elements where each element has a numerical index starting from 0. The intuition: arrays allow us to store multiple values in sequence and access them by position. This is prerequisite because our algorithm works with array indices to track elements. Key operations: 1. Access: array[i] gets element at index i. 2. Iteration: loop through indices to process all elements. 3. Length: len(array) gives number of elements. Arrays are fundamental because the 3Sum problem input is an array, and we need indices to avoid reusing elements. This feeds into L4 as the source of keys and values for the dictionary. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (indices from 0 to n-1), confusing index with value, and not understanding that arrays maintain order. The real code pattern: arr=[10,20,30]; x=arr[0]; # x=10; for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i]).

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has 3 elements. Step 2: Index 0 refers to first element: 5. Step 3: Index 1 refers to second element: 10. Step 4: Index 2 refers to third element: 15.
Step 1: Count elements: 100 and 200 → length=2. Step 2: Indices: 0 for 100, 1 for 200. Step 3: Last index is length-1 = 1.

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n²)
approaches.summary: Sort the array first, then fix one element and use two pointers to find pairs in the remaining sorted subarray that sum to the negative of the fixed element, skipping duplicates by checking adjacent elements. This is optimal for typical constraints.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space if ignoring output storage, or O(k) for output

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Unique Triplets Summing to Zero Using Two Pointer Approach
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all unique triplets in an array that sum to zero using sorting and two-pointer technique.
ladders.explanation: The 3Sum problem requires finding all unique triplets (i, j, k) in an array such that nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] = 0. The optimal approach involves sorting the array first, which takes O(n log n) time. Then, for each element nums[i] as the fixed element, we need to find pairs (j, k) in the remaining subarray (i+1 to n-1) that sum to -nums[i]. This reduces to a two-sum problem on a sorted array, which can be solved efficiently using two pointers. The left pointer starts at i+1, and the right pointer starts at the end of the array. If the sum of nums[left] + nums[right] is less than -nums[i], we move the left pointer right to increase the sum. If it's greater, we move the right pointer left to decrease the sum. When we find a match, we add the triplet to the result. To avoid duplicates, we skip over duplicate values for nums[i], nums[left], and nums[right] by checking adjacent elements after finding a valid triplet or when moving pointers. The overall time complexity is O(n²) due to the nested loops (outer loop for i and inner while loop for two pointers), and space complexity is O(1) extra space (ignoring output storage). This approach is efficient for typical constraints where n ≤ 3000. The intuition is that sorting allows us to use the two-pointer technique to find pairs in linear time for each fixed element, and skipping duplicates ensures uniqueness without needing a set. Common mistakes include not sorting the array first, which breaks the two-pointer logic, forgetting to skip duplicates leading to duplicate triplets in the output, and incorrect pointer movements. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the key subroutine of finding pairs with two pointers for each fixed element.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort the array: [-4, -1, -1, 0, 1, 2]. Step 2: Iterate i from 0 to n-3. For i=0, nums[i]=-4, target = 4. Set left=1, right=5. While left<right: nums[left]=-1, nums[right]=2, sum=1 < 4, move left to 2. nums[left]=-1, nums[right]=2, sum=1 < 4, move left to 3. nums[left]=0, nums[right]=2, sum=2 < 4, move left to 4. nums[left]=1, nums[right]=2, sum=3 < 4, move left to 5, loop ends. For i=1, nums[i]=-1, target=1. Skip duplicate? nums[1]=-1, nums[0]=-4, not duplicate, proceed. left=2, right=5. nums[left]=-1, nums[right]=2, sum=1 == target, add triplet [-1,-1,2]. Skip duplicates for left and right: move left to 3 (nums[3]=0 ≠ -1), move right to 4 (nums[4]=1 ≠ 2). left=3, right=4: nums[left]=0, nums[right]=1, sum=1 == target, add triplet [-1,0,1]. Skip duplicates: move left to 4, loop ends. For i=2, nums[i]=-1, check if duplicate with nums[1]=-1, skip. For i=3, nums[i]=0, target=0. left=4, right=5: nums[left]=1, nums[right]=2, sum=3 > 0, move right to 4, loop ends. For i=4, nums[i]=1, target=-1, left=5, right=5, loop ends. Result: [[-1,-1,2],[-1,0,1]].

Step 1: Sort the array: [0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Iterate i from 0 to n-3. For i=0, nums[i]=0, target=0. left=1, right=3. While left<right: nums[left]=0, nums[right]=0, sum=0 == target, add triplet [0,0,0]. Skip duplicates for left and right: move left to 2 (nums[2]=0, skip as duplicate), move right to 2 (nums[2]=0, skip), loop ends. For i=1, nums[i]=0, check if duplicate with nums[0]=0, skip. For i=2, nums[i]=0, check duplicate with nums[1]=0, skip. Result: [[0,0,0]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find All Pairs in Sorted Array Summing to Target Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array and a target sum, find all unique pairs (i,j) where i<j and nums[i]+nums[j]=target.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the 3Sum solution. After sorting the array and fixing one element in 3Sum, we need to find pairs in the remaining sorted subarray that sum to a specific target (which is the negative of the fixed element). The two-pointer technique is ideal for this because the array is sorted. We initialize two pointers: left at the start of the subarray and right at the end. While left < right, we compute the current sum = nums[left] + nums[right]. If the sum equals the target, we record the pair. Then, to avoid duplicates, we move both pointers inward, skipping any duplicate values by incrementing left while nums[left] equals its previous value and decrementing right while nums[right] equals its previous value. If the sum is less than the target, we increment left to increase the sum (since the array is sorted in non-decreasing order). If the sum is greater than the target, we decrement right to decrease the sum. This process continues until left meets right. The time complexity is O(n) for a subarray of length n, as each pointer moves at most n times. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. The intuition is that sorting allows us to efficiently navigate towards the target sum by adjusting pointers based on whether the current sum is too low or too high. This is similar to binary search but for two elements. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates correctly, which can lead to duplicate pairs in the output, and incorrect pointer updates that might skip valid pairs. The connection to L0 is that this sub-routine is called for each fixed element in the outer loop of 3Sum, making the overall solution O(n²).

examples.trace: Step 1: Sorted array: [1,2,3,4,5]. left=0, right=4. Step 2: nums[left]=1, nums[right]=5, sum=6 == target, add pair [1,5]. Skip duplicates: left=1 (nums[1]=2 ≠ 1), right=3 (nums[3]=4 ≠ 5). Step 3: left=1, right=3: nums[left]=2, nums[right]=4, sum=6 == target, add pair [2,4]. Skip duplicates: left=2 (nums[2]=3 ≠ 2), right=2 (nums[2]=3 ≠ 4), loop ends. Result: [[1,5],[2,4]].

Step 1: Sorted array: [0,0,2,2]. left=0, right=3. Step 2: nums[left]=0, nums[right]=2, sum=2 == target, add pair [0,2]. Skip duplicates: increment left to 1 (nums[1]=0, duplicate, skip to 2), decrement right to 2 (nums[2]=2, duplicate, skip to 1), now left=2, right=1, loop ends. Result: [[0,2]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two Pointer Technique on Sorted Array for Pair Sum
ladders.desc: Use two pointers to find a pair in a sorted array that sums to a target, moving pointers based on comparison with target.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer technique is a fundamental algorithm for solving problems on sorted arrays, especially for finding pairs with a given sum. The core logic involves initializing two indices: one at the beginning (left) and one at the end (right) of the array. Since the array is sorted in non-decreasing order, we can exploit this property to efficiently search for pairs. At each step, compute the sum of the elements at left and right. Compare this sum to the target. If the sum equals the target, we have found a valid pair. If the sum is less than the target, we need a larger sum; because the array is sorted, moving the left pointer to the right will increase the sum (as elements to the right are larger or equal). Conversely, if the sum is greater than the target, we need a smaller sum; moving the right pointer to the left will decrease the sum (as elements to the left are smaller or equal). We repeat this process until the pointers meet (left >= right). This technique runs in O(n) time since each pointer moves at most n times, and uses O(1) extra space. The intuition is that sorting transforms the problem from a brute-force O(n²) search to a linear scan by allowing intelligent pointer movements. It's similar to the idea of converging from both ends towards a solution. Common mistakes include not sorting the array first, which invalidates the pointer movement logic, and incorrect pointer updates that could skip over valid pairs or cause infinite loops. The connection to L1 is that this core logic is extended in the sub-routine to handle multiple pairs and skip duplicates.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sorted array: [1,3,5,7,9]. left=0, right=4. Step 2: nums[left]=1, nums[right]=9, sum=10 == target, return [1,9].
Step 1: Sorted array: [2,4,6,8]. left=0, right=3. Step 2: nums[left]=2, nums[right]=8, sum=10 == target, return [2,8].

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting an Array and Iterating with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Sort an array and use nested loops to iterate over elements, fixing one element and processing the rest.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves two key operations: sorting an array and then using nested loops to traverse it. Sorting is typically done with efficient algorithms like quicksort or mergesort, which have O(n log n) time complexity. Once sorted, the array is in non-decreasing order, which enables optimized search techniques like the two-pointer method. In the context of 3Sum, after sorting, we use an outer loop to fix one element (say nums[i]) and an inner loop to process the remaining elements (from i+1 to end). The outer loop runs from index 0 to n-3 to ensure at least three elements are available. For each fixed element, we can apply algorithms on the subarray to find pairs. This structure reduces the problem from a triple nested loop (O(n³)) to a double nested loop (O(n²)) when combined with linear-time subroutines. The intuition is that sorting brings order to the data, allowing us to avoid redundant checks and use efficient search strategies. Common mistakes include sorting in-place without considering if the original array needs preservation, incorrect loop bounds leading to index errors, and not skipping duplicate fixed elements which can cause duplicate triplets. Time complexity for sorting is O(n log n), and for nested loops, it's O(n²) in worst case. Space complexity is O(1) if sorting in-place, or O(n) if using extra space for sorting. The connection to L2 is that this sorted array and loop structure sets up the environment for applying the two-pointer technique in the inner processing.

examples.output: Sorted: [1,2,3], outer i=0 to 0, inner j=1 to 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Sort nums: [1,2,3]. Step 2: Outer loop i from 0 to n-3 = 0. For i=0, nums[i]=1. Inner loop j from i+1=1 to n-1=2: j=1 nums[j]=2, j=2 nums[j]=3.

examples.output: Sorted: [-1,0,1], outer i=0 to 0, inner j=1 to 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Sort nums: [-1,0,1]. Step 2: Outer loop i from 0 to n-3 = 0. For i=0, nums[i]=-1. Inner loop j from i+1=1 to n-1=2: j=1 nums[j]=0, j=2 nums[j]=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Moving Array Indices
ladders.desc: Compare values at array indices and increment or decrement indices based on conditions.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves working with array indices to access elements, compare their values, and update the indices. In programming, an array is a contiguous block of memory where elements are accessed via indices (usually starting from 0). To compare values, we use expressions like nums[i] < nums[j] or nums[left] + nums[right] == target. Based on the comparison, we move indices: incrementing (e.g., i += 1) moves to the next element, decrementing (e.g., j -= 1) moves to the previous element. This is fundamental in algorithms that traverse arrays, such as loops or two-pointer techniques. The intuition is that by controlling indices, we can scan through the array in a controlled manner, skipping elements or converging towards a solution. For example, in a sorted array, if the sum of two elements is too low, incrementing the left index increases the sum because later elements are larger. Conversely, if the sum is too high, decrementing the right index decreases the sum. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors (e.g., accessing nums[n] when indices go from 0 to n-1), off-by-one errors in loop conditions, and not updating indices correctly in while loops leading to infinite loops. Time complexity for these operations is O(1) per comparison or move, but in algorithms, they are repeated many times. Space complexity is O(1) as only a few integer variables are used. The connection to L3 is that this operation is used within the nested loops to iterate over the array and apply comparisons for the two-pointer logic.

examples.output: Compare nums[i]=1 and nums[j]=3, then i+=1 to 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Access nums[0]=1, nums[2]=3. Step 2: Compare 1 < 3, true. Step 3: Increment i from 0 to 1.

examples.output: Sum nums[left]+nums[right]=5+15=20 == target, then left+=1 to 1, right-=1 to 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Access nums[0]=5, nums[2]=15. Step 2: Compute sum 5+15=20. Step 3: Compare sum == target (20==20), true. Step 4: Increment left from 0 to 1, decrement right from 2 to 1.

testCases.expected: Sum: 20==20, move left to 1, right to 1
Compare: 0==0, move i to 1, j to 0
Sum: 10+20=30==30, move left to 1, right to 0
Compare: 5==5, move i to 1, j to 1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Index Operations
ladders.desc: Understand what an array is, how to access elements by index, and perform simple operations like reading and comparing values.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations. Each element is accessed by an index, which is an integer starting from 0 in most programming languages. For example, in an array nums = [10, 20, 30], nums[0] is 10, nums[1] is 20, and nums[2] is 30. The length of the array, often denoted as n, is the number of elements it contains. Basic operations include reading an element at a given index (e.g., x = nums[i]), writing or updating an element (e.g., nums[i] = 5), and iterating over all elements using a loop. Comparisons between elements are done using relational operators: == for equality, < for less than, > for greater than, etc. For instance, nums[0] < nums[1] checks if the first element is less than the second. These operations are essential for any algorithm that processes arrays. The intuition is that arrays provide random access, meaning any element can be accessed in constant time O(1), making them efficient for many tasks. Common mistakes include using indices outside the valid range (0 to n-1), which causes errors like index out of bounds, and confusing zero-based indexing with one-based indexing. Time complexity for access and comparison is O(1). Space complexity for the array itself is O(n) to store n elements. The connection to L4 is that this foundation is used to perform comparisons and move indices in more advanced algorithms like the two-pointer technique.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array nums has indices 0,1,2. Step 2: Access element at index 1: nums[1] = 3.
Step 1: Access nums[0]=1, nums[2]=3. Step 2: Compare 1 < 3 using the < operator. Step 3: Result is true.
